preamble.summary: The pivot word 'but now' (ve-attah) marks the devastating shift from Job's remembered glory in chapter 29 to his present humiliation. Men younger than Job — men whose fathers Job would not have placed with the dogs guarding his flock — now mock him openly. These are social outcasts, gaunt with hunger, gnawing roots in the wasteland, driven from human community. Yet even they spit at Job and treat him with contempt. God has loosened Job's bowstring and afflicted him; mockers assault him, they tear up his path, they advance like troops through a breach. Terrors overwhelm him; his dignity is blown away like wind; his prosperity vanishes like a cloud. Now his soul is poured out, pain seizes his bones at night, his garment is disfigured by disease, God has thrown him into the mud. Job cries to God but receives no answer — he stands in the assembly and screams for help. He has become a brother to jackals and a companion to ostriches. His skin blackens and peels; his bones burn with fever. His harp is tuned to mourning, his flute to the sound of weeping.
preamble.remarkable: The rhetorical structure of chapter 30 is built on devastation by contrast. Every element of honor from chapter 29 is systematically demolished. Where elders once stood at Job's approach (29:8), now the dregs of society spit in his face (30:10). Where Job once wore righteousness as a robe (29:14), now disease disfigures his garment (30:18). Where Job once comforted mourners (29:25), now he cries out and no one comforts him. The 'but now' (ve-attah) that opens the chapter (vv. 1, 9, 16) creates a three-panel structure of degradation: social humiliation (1-15), divine assault (16-23), and existential despair (24-31). The description of the outcasts in verses 1-8 is the most detailed portrait of extreme poverty in the Hebrew Bible — people reduced to eating roots, living in wadis, braying like donkeys among the bushes. These are not the conventional poor whom Job once helped; they are the expelled, the nameless. That even they look down on Job measures the depth of his fall.
preamble.friction: Job's contemptuous description of his mockers (vv. 1-8) creates an uncomfortable tension with his earlier claim to have been 'father to the destitute' (29:16). Was Job's compassion limited to the respectable poor? The text may be revealing an honest human limitation: even a righteous person can harbor class prejudice. Alternatively, Job may be describing these people not with contempt but with accuracy — they are genuinely degraded human beings, and the point is that even the most degraded now stand above him. The theological weight falls on verses 20-23, where Job accuses God directly: 'I cry to you and you do not answer me... you have turned cruel to me.' This is not complaint about suffering but accusation of divine cruelty — a charge the book never fully resolves through human argument but only through theophany.
preamble.connections: Job's cry 'I cry to you and you do not answer' (v20) echoes through the psalms of lament (Psalm 22:2, 88:1-2) and anticipates Jesus's cry of dereliction from the cross (Matthew 27:46). The image of being thrown into the mud (v19) anticipates Jeremiah's experience of being lowered into a cistern of mire (Jeremiah 38:6). Job becoming 'a brother to jackals' (v29) parallels Micah 1:8 where the prophet makes lamentation 'like the jackals.' The transformation of the harp to mourning and the flute to weeping (v31) reverses the celebration imagery of Isaiah 24:8-9 and anticipates the exiles hanging their harps on willows in Psalm 137:2. The entire chapter is the anti-psalm: where psalms of thanksgiving move from lament to praise, Job 30 moves from praise (ch. 29) to lament with no resolution.

verses.text_hebrew: וְעַתָּ֤ה שָֽׂחֲק֣וּ עָלַי֮ צְעִירִ֥ים מִמֶּ֗נִּי לְיָ֫מִ֥ים אֲשֶׁר־מָאַ֥סְתִּי אֲבוֹתָ֑ם לָ֝שִׁ֗ית עִם־כַּלְבֵ֥י צֹאנִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: But now they that are younger than I have me in derision, whose fathers I would have disdained to have set with the dogs of my flock.
verses.rendering:
But now they laugh at me —
men younger than I,
whose fathers I would not have placed
among the dogs that guard my flock.
translator_notes: Ve-attah ('but now') is the hinge word of the entire monologue — it marks the break between past honor and present degradation. The tse'irim (younger men) mocking Job inverts the deference of 29:8. Job's comment about their fathers and the dogs is not casual insult but a precise social measurement: these men's fathers were so unreliable that Job would not have trusted them with the lowest task on his estate.

verses.text_hebrew: גַּם־כֹּ֣חַ יְ֭דֵיהֶם לָ֣מָּה לִּ֑י עָ֝לֵ֗ימוֹ אָ֣בַד כָּֽלַח׃
verses.text_kjv: Yea, whereto might the strength of their hands profit me, in whom old age was perished?
verses.rendering:
The strength of their hands — what use was it to me?
All vigor had perished from them.
translator_notes: The kelach ('vigor, mature strength') that has perished from these men may refer to premature aging from deprivation. Job is describing people so broken by poverty that even their physical labor was worthless. The rhetorical question lammah li ('what is it to me?') emphasizes their uselessness.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּחֶ֥סֶר וּבְכָפָ֗ן גַּ֫לְמ֥וּד הַֽעֹרְקִ֥ים צִיָּ֑ה אֶ֝֗מֶשׁ שׁוֹאָ֥ה וּמְשֹׁאָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: For want and famine they were solitary; fleeing into the wilderness in former time desolate and waste.
verses.rendering:
Gaunt with want and hunger,
they gnawed the dry ground —
the dark wasteland of desolation and ruin.
translator_notes: The verse piles up terms of deprivation: cheser (want, lack), kafan (famine, hunger), galmud (barren, stripped bare). The verb ha-orqim ('gnawing, fleeing to') combined with tsiyah (dry land, parched ground) creates an image of people so desperate they chew the dust of the wilderness. The phrase emesh sho'ah u-mesho'ah ('yesterday's desolation and destruction') suggests an already-ruined landscape.

verses.text_hebrew: הַקֹּטְפִ֣ים מַלּ֣וּחַ עֲלֵי־שִׂ֑יחַ וְשֹׁ֖רֶשׁ רְתָמִ֣ים לַחְמָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: Who cut up mallows by the bushes, and juniper roots for their meat.
verses.rendering:
They pluck salt-herb among the shrubs,
and broom-root is their bread.
translator_notes: The malluach (salt-plant, salt-herb) is a bitter, barely edible plant growing in arid regions. The retamim (broom plant) has roots that are fibrous and nearly inedible. These are starvation foods — the last resort of people who have exhausted all normal sources of sustenance. The word lachmam ('their bread, their food') emphasizes that this is not a supplement but their only sustenance.

verses.text_hebrew: מִן־גֵּ֥ו יְגֹרָ֑שׁוּ יָרִ֥יעוּ עָ֝לֵ֗ימוֹ כַּגַּנָּֽב׃
verses.text_kjv: They were driven forth from among men, (they cried after them as after a thief;)
verses.rendering:
They are driven out from the community;
people shout after them as after a thief.
translator_notes: The verb yegorashu ('they are driven out') is the same verb used for expulsion from Eden (Genesis 3:24) and Cain's banishment (Genesis 4:14). These outcasts are expelled from gev (the midst, the community). The comparison to a thief (gannav) means they are hounded and pursued even after being cast out — society treats them as criminals, not merely as unfortunates.

verses.text_hebrew: בַּעֲר֣וּץ נְחָלִ֣ים לִשְׁכֹּ֑ן חֹרֵ֖י עָפָ֣ר וְכֵפִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: To dwell in the cliffs of the valleys, in caves of the earth, and in the rocks.
verses.rendering:
They live in the gullies of the wadis,
in holes in the ground and among the rocks.
translator_notes: The aruts nechalim ('ravines of the wadis') are the eroded channels of seasonal streams — dangerous during flash floods, desolate the rest of the year. The chorei afar ('holes of dust/earth') and kefim ('rocks, crags') describe subhuman dwelling conditions. These are not homes but hiding places.

verses.text_hebrew: בֵּ֣ין שִׂ֭יחִים יִנְהָ֑קוּ תַּ֥חַת חָ֝ר֗וּל יְסֻפָּֽחוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Among the bushes they brayed; under the nettles they were gathered together.
verses.rendering:
They bray among the bushes;
they huddle together beneath the nettles.
translator_notes: The verb yinhaqu ('they bray') is the sound a wild donkey makes — these people have been reduced to animal noises and animal behavior. The charul (nettles, thorny weeds) provides their only shelter. The verb yesuppachu ('they are gathered together, they huddle') suggests clinging to one another for warmth or safety in their miserable refuge.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּנֵֽי־נָ֭בָל גַּם־בְּנֵ֣י בְלִי־שֵׁ֑ם נִ֝כְּא֗וּ מִן־הָאָֽרֶץ׃
verses.text_kjv: They were children of fools, yea, children of base men: they were viler than the earth.
verses.rendering:
Sons of the senseless, sons of the nameless —
whipped out of the land.
translator_notes: Bene naval ('sons of the fool/senseless one') and bene veli-shem ('sons of no-name') are social designations: these are people with no lineage, no reputation, no place in the social registry. The verb nikke'u ('they were beaten, struck, driven out') combined with min ha-arets ('from the land') describes total social erasure — they have been flogged out of civilization itself.

verses.text_hebrew: וְ֭עַתָּה נְגִינָתָ֣ם הָיִ֑יתִי וָאֱהִ֖י לָהֶ֣ם לְמִלָּֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: And now am I their song, yea, I am their byword.
verses.rendering:
But now I have become their mocking song;
I have become their byword.
translator_notes: The second ve-attah ('but now') opens the second panel of degradation. The neginatam ('their song, their taunt-song') means Job is the subject of their crude entertainment. The millah ('word, byword') means his name has become a proverb for misfortune. The man who once silenced nobles with his speech (29:9-10) is now the punchline of the lowest people on earth.

verses.text_hebrew: תִּֽ֭עֲבוּנִי רָ֣חֲקוּ מֶ֑נִּי וּ֝מִפָּנַ֗י לֹא־חָשְׂכ֥וּ רֹֽק׃
verses.text_kjv: They abhor me, they flee far from me, and spare not to spit in my face.
verses.rendering:
They despise me and keep their distance;
they do not hold back from spitting in my face.
translator_notes: The verb ti'avuni ('they find me abhorrent, they abhor me') expresses visceral disgust. The paradox is sharp: they distance themselves from Job in revulsion but come close enough to spit (roq) at his face. Spitting in the face was the supreme insult in the ancient Near East (Numbers 12:14, Deuteronomy 25:9). The man whose face once radiated light (29:24) now has spit on it.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּֽי־יִתְר֣וֹ פִ֭תַּח וַיְעַנֵּ֑נִי וְ֝רֶ֗סֶן מִפָּנַ֥י שִׁלֵּֽחוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Because he hath loosed my cord, and afflicted me, they have also let loose the bridle before me.
verses.rendering:
Because God has loosened my bowstring and humbled me,
they have cast off all restraint before me.
translator_notes: The yitro (his cord, his bowstring) that God has loosened refers back to Job's bow that was renewed in his hand (29:20). God has unstrung Job's weapon — removed his power. The resen (bridle, restraint) that others have cast off means that with Job's power gone, social restraints have dissolved. People who would never have dared insult him now do so freely because God has removed his protection.

verses.text_hebrew: עַל־יָמִין֮ פִּרְחַ֪ח יָ֫ק֥וּמוּ רַגְלַ֥י שִׁלֵּ֑חוּ וַיָּסֹ֥לּוּ עָ֝לַ֗י אׇרְח֥וֹת אֵידָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: Upon my right hand rise the youth; they push away my feet, and they raise up against me the ways of their destruction.
translator_notes: The pirchach (brood, rabble — a contemptuous term for young upstarts) rise on Job's right side, which was traditionally the place of the defender or advocate in a legal setting. They push away his feet (metaphor for undermining his standing) and build orchot eidam ('roads/ramps of their destruction') — siege imagery suggesting a military assault on Job's remaining dignity.

verses.text_hebrew: נָתְס֣וּ נְתִיבָתִ֑י לְהַוָּתִ֖י יֹעִ֣ילוּ לֹ֣א עֹזֵ֥ר לָֽמוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: They mar my path, they set forward my calamity, they have no helper.
translator_notes: The verb natsu ('they tore up, broke apart') applied to netivati ('my path') means they have destroyed Job's way forward. The havvati ('my destruction, my calamity') becomes their gain — yo'ilu ('they profit, they benefit'). The final phrase lo ozer lamo ('no helper for them' or 'they have no one restraining them') is ambiguous: it may mean no one stops them, or that even they — these wretched people — have no allies, yet they still prevail over Job.

verses.text_hebrew: כְּפֶ֣רֶץ רָחָ֣ב יֶאֱתָ֑יוּ תַּ֥חַת שׁ֝וֹאָ֗ה הִתְגַּלְגָּֽלוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: They came upon me as a wide breaking in of waters: in the desolation they rolled themselves upon me.
verses.rendering:
They pour through like a wide breach;
amid the crash of ruin they roll over me.
translator_notes: The perets rachav ('wide breach') is a military image — a gap smashed through a city wall through which attackers pour. The verb hitgalgalu ('they rolled, tumbled') describes the chaotic rush of an army flooding through a broken wall. Job is a besieged city whose defenses have been breached, and the attackers are trampling everything inside.

verses.text_hebrew: הׇהְפַּ֥ךְ עָלַ֗י בַּלָּ֫ה֥וֹת תִּרְדֹּ֣ף כָּ֭רוּחַ נְדִ֣בָתִ֑י וּ֝כְעָ֗ב עָבְרָ֥ה יְשֻׁעָתִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Terrors are turned upon me: they pursue my soul as the wind: and my welfare passeth away as a cloud.
translator_notes: The ballahot (terrors) are personified — they are turned (hahpakh) against Job like an army redirected. His nedivati ('my nobility, my generous spirit, my dignity') is pursued ka-ruach ('like wind') — it is insubstantial, uncatchable, gone. His yeshu'ati ('my salvation, my well-being, my prosperity') passes ke-av ('like a cloud') — visible for a moment, then dissolved. Wind and cloud: both images of what cannot be held.

verses.text_hebrew: וְעַתָּ֗ה עָ֭לַי תִּשְׁתַּפֵּ֣ךְ נַפְשִׁ֑י יֹ֖אחֲז֣וּנִי יְמֵי־עֹֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: And now my soul is poured out upon me; the days of affliction have taken hold upon me.
verses.rendering:
But now my soul is poured out within me;
days of affliction have seized me.
translator_notes: The third ve-attah ('but now') opens the final and most personal panel. The verb tishtappekh ('is poured out') describes Job's nephesh (self, soul, life-force) as a liquid being emptied — the same image in Psalm 42:4 ('I pour out my soul within me') and Lamentations 2:12 (children's lives poured out). The yeme-oni ('days of affliction') have seized Job — they grip him and will not release.

verses.text_hebrew: לַ֗יְלָה עֲ֭צָמַי נִקַּ֣ר מֵעָלָ֑י וְ֝עֹרְקַ֗י לֹ֣א יִשְׁכָּבֽוּן׃
verses.text_kjv: My bones are pierced in me in the night season: and my sinews that gnaw me take no rest.
verses.rendering:
At night my bones are pierced through;
my gnawing pains never rest.
translator_notes: The laylah (night) — once the time when dew rested on Job's branches (29:19) — is now the time of agony. His atsamai (bones) are niqqar ('pierced, bored through'), describing deep skeletal pain. The orqai ('my gnawing pains' or 'my sinews') lo yishkavun ('do not lie down, do not rest') — the pain is relentless, it does not sleep even when Job tries to.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּרׇב־כֹּ֭חַ יִתְחַפֵּ֣שׂ לְבוּשִׁ֑י כְּפִ֖י כֻתׇּנְתִּ֣י יַאַזְרֵֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: By the great force of my disease is my garment changed: it bindeth me about as the collar of my coat.
verses.rendering:
With great force my garment is disfigured;
it grips me tight like the collar of my tunic.
translator_notes: The verb yitchappes ('is changed, is disfigured, is disguised') applied to levushi ('my garment') likely means Job's clothing is stiffened and distorted by the discharge from his sores. Where he once wore righteousness as a robe (29:14), now disease reshapes his clothing. The kefi kuttanti ('like the mouth/collar of my tunic') binding him suggests the garment has become a second skin of suffering, constricting rather than covering.

verses.text_hebrew: הֹרָ֥נִי לַחֹ֑מֶר וָ֝אֶתְמַשֵּׁ֗ל כֶּעָפָ֥ר וָאֵֽפֶר׃
verses.text_kjv: He hath cast me into the mire, and I am become like dust and ashes.
verses.rendering:
He has thrown me into the mud,
and I have become like dust and ashes.
translator_notes: The subject of horani ('he has thrown me, he has cast me') is God — implicit but unmistakable. The chomer (mud, clay, mire) is the substance from which humans were formed (Job 10:9), but here it is a place of degradation, not creation. The afar va-efer ('dust and ashes') is the same phrase Abraham uses in Genesis 18:27 to describe human insignificance before God, and it appears in Job 42:6 at the book's resolution.

key_terms.semantic_range: dust and ashes; a merism for complete insignificance, mortality, and abasement
key_terms.note: This phrase bookends Job's journey: he sits in ashes in chapter 2, declares himself dust and ashes in chapter 30, and uses the same phrase in his final response to God in 42:6. It functions as Job's self-identification in suffering.

verses.text_hebrew: אֲשַׁוַּ֣ע אֵ֭לֶיךָ וְלֹ֣א תַעֲנֵ֑נִי עָ֝מַ֗דְתִּי וַתִּתְבֹּ֥נֶן בִּֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: I cry unto thee, and thou dost not hear me: I stand up, and thou regardest me not.
verses.rendering:
I cry out to you, and you do not answer me;
I stand before you, and you only stare at me.
verses.expanded_rendering: This verse is the emotional center of chapter 30 and one of the most raw prayers in the Hebrew Bible. Job addresses God directly — ashawe'a elekha ('I scream to you') — using the intense verb for a desperate cry. The response is silence: lo ta'aneni ('you do not answer me'). Then Job stands (amadti) in the posture of formal petition, and God's response is worse than silence: va-titbonen bi ('you consider me, you stare at me'). God looks at Job but does nothing. The combination of divine attention and divine inaction is more devastating than pure absence would be.
translator_notes: The verb shava (cry, scream for help) is among the most urgent in Hebrew prayer vocabulary — it is the cry of the oppressed in Exodus 2:23. God answered that cry; God does not answer this one. The verb hitbonen ('to gaze, to consider carefully') implies God is watching Job with full awareness but choosing not to intervene. This is not a hidden God but a God who is present, watching, and silent.

verses.text_hebrew: תֵּהָפֵ֣ךְ לְאַכְזָ֣ר לִ֑י בְּעֹ֥צֶם יָ֝דְךָ֗ תִשְׂטְמֵֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou art become cruel to me: with thy strong hand thou opposest thyself against me.
verses.rendering:
You have turned cruel against me;
with the full force of your hand you attack me.
translator_notes: The verb tehapekh ('you turn, you change') combined with le-akhzar ('to cruelty, into a cruel one') is Job's most direct accusation: God has changed character. The b'otsom yadkha ('with the might of your hand') is the same language used for God's mighty acts of salvation in the exodus (Exodus 6:1). The hand that once rescued Israel now persecutes Job. The verb tistmeni ('you oppose me, you bear a grudge against me') suggests sustained hostility, not a single act.

verses.text_hebrew: תִּשָּׂאֵ֣נִי אֶל־ר֭וּחַ תַּרְכִּיבֵ֑נִי וּ֝תְמֹגְגֵ֗נִי תּוּשִׁיָּֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou liftest me up to the wind; thou causest me to ride upon it, and dissolvest my substance.
verses.rendering:
You lift me up on the wind and drive me before it;
you dissolve me in the storm.
translator_notes: God lifts Job into the ruach (wind) — not gently but to be battered. The verb tarkiveni ('you cause me to ride') uses riding imagery but without any sense of control or mastery — Job is mounted on the storm against his will. The verb temogegeni ('you dissolve me, you melt me') describes the disintegration of his tushiyyah ('substance, sound wisdom, essential being'). Job is being unmade.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּֽי־יָ֭דַעְתִּי מָ֣וֶת תְּשִׁיבֵ֑נִי וּבֵ֖ית מוֹעֵ֣ד לְכׇל־חָֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: For I know that thou wilt bring me to death, and to the house appointed for all living.
verses.rendering:
I know you will return me to death,
to the house appointed for every living thing.
translator_notes: The mavet (death) is Job's destination, and God is the one driving him there — teshiveni ('you will return me, you will bring me back'). The beit mo'ed le-khol chai ('house of meeting/appointment for all the living') is Sheol, the grave, described not as punishment but as universal appointment. Every living creature has this destination. The word mo'ed ('appointed time, meeting place') gives death the character of a scheduled event — God has set the date.

verses.text_hebrew: אַ֗ךְ לֹא־בְ֭עִי יִשְׁלַח־יָ֑ד אִם־בְּ֝פִיד֗וֹ לָהֶ֥ן שֽׁוּעַ׃
verses.text_kjv: Howbeit he will not stretch out his hand to the grave, though they cry in his destruction.
verses.rendering:
Surely a drowning man reaches out his hand;
surely in his disaster he cries for help.
translator_notes: This verse is notoriously difficult in Hebrew. The general sense appears to be: does not a person in a heap of ruins (be'i) reach out his hand? Does not someone in catastrophe (pid) cry out (shua')? Job is defending his right to cry out — if even a person buried in rubble stretches out a hand for rescue, how much more is Job justified in crying to God from the rubble of his life.

verses.text_hebrew: אִם־לֹ֣א בָ֭כִיתִי לִקְשֵׁה־י֑וֹם עָֽגְמָ֥ה נַ֝פְשִׁ֗י לָאֶבְיֽוֹן׃
verses.text_kjv: Did not I weep for him that was in trouble? was not my soul grieved for the poor?
verses.rendering:
Did I not weep for the one whose days were hard?
Was my soul not grieved for the poor?
translator_notes: Job now turns to his record of compassion as evidence. He wept for the qesheh-yom ('the hard of day,' the person experiencing difficult times) and his nephesh was agmah ('grieved, distressed') for the evyon (the destitute). This verse connects backward to 29:12-17 (Job's social ethic) and forward to the logic of chapter 31: if Job practiced compassion, he should receive compassion.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י ט֣וֹב קִ֭וִּיתִי וַיָּ֣בֹא רָ֑ע וַאֲיַחֲלָ֖ה לְא֣וֹר וַיָּ֣בֹא אֹֽפֶל׃
verses.text_kjv: When I looked for good, then evil came unto me: and when I waited for light, there came darkness.
verses.rendering:
I hoped for good, but evil came;
I waited for light, but darkness arrived.
translator_notes: The antithetical parallelism is perfect: tov/ra (good/evil) and or/ofel (light/deep darkness). The verbs qivviti ('I hoped, I waited expectantly') and ayachalah ('I waited with longing') describe patient, expectant trust — the kind of trust the retribution theology promises will be rewarded. Instead, the opposite arrived. This is the sharpest statement of the book's central problem: righteous expectation met by inexplicable reversal.

verses.text_hebrew: מֵעַ֣י רֻ֭תְּחוּ וְלֹ֣א דָמּ֑וּ קִדְּמֻ֖נִי יְמֵי־עֹֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: My bowels boiled, and rested not: the days of affliction prevented me.
verses.rendering:
My insides churn without rest;
days of affliction confront me.
translator_notes: The me'ai (my intestines, my inner organs) ruttachu ('are boiled, are in turmoil') — in Hebrew physiology, the intestines are the seat of deep emotion, roughly equivalent to 'my gut is in an uproar.' The verb lo damu ('they do not rest, they are not quiet') emphasizes relentlessness. The yeme-oni ('days of affliction') qiddamuni ('confronted me, came to meet me') — affliction came out to ambush Job on the road.

verses.text_hebrew: קֹדֵ֣ר הִ֭לַּכְתִּי בְּלֹ֣א חַמָּ֑ה ק֖וּמְתִּי בַקָּהָ֣ל אֲשַׁוֵּֽעַ׃
verses.text_kjv: I went mourning without the sun: I stood up, and I cried in the congregation.
verses.rendering:
I go about blackened, but not by the sun;
I rise in the assembly and cry for help.
translator_notes: Qoder ('blackened, dark, mourning') describes Job's skin condition — he is darkened, but not from sunburn (belo chammah, 'without the sun'). The darkening is from disease. He rises in the qahal (assembly, congregation) and screams (ashawe'a) — the same verb from verse 20. The man who once presided over the assembly in honor (29:7-10) now stands in it screaming in desperation, and no one responds.

verses.text_hebrew: אָ֣ח הָ֭יִיתִי לְתַנִּ֑ים וְ֝רֵ֗עַ לִבְנ֥וֹת יַעֲנָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: I am a brother to dragons, and a companion to owls.
verses.rendering:
I have become a brother to jackals,
a companion to ostriches.
translator_notes: The tannim (jackals) and benot ya'anah (daughters of the ostrich, or ostriches) are animals of the wilderness and of ruins — they inhabit destroyed cities (Isaiah 13:21-22, 34:13). By calling them 'brother' (ach) and 'companion' (re'a), Job says his only kinship now is with desolation creatures. The man who once sat among elders and nobles now keeps company with scavengers of the wasteland.

verses.text_hebrew: עוֹרִ֣י שָׁחַ֣ר מֵעָלָ֑י וְ֝עַצְמִ֗י חָ֣רָה מִנִּי־חֹֽרֶב׃
verses.text_kjv: My skin is black upon me, and my bones are burned with heat.
verses.rendering:
My skin turns black and peels from me;
my bones burn with fever.
translator_notes: The ori (my skin) shachor ('is black, is darkened') connects to 'blackened' in verse 28. The verb me'alai ('from upon me') suggests the skin is peeling away. The atsamai (my bones) charah ('burned, were hot') from chorev ('heat, dryness, fever') describes the sensation of internal burning — the disease attacks both surface (skin) and core (bones).

verses.text_hebrew: וַיְהִ֣י לְ֭אֵבֶל כִּנֹּרִ֑י וְ֝עֻגָבִ֗י לְק֣וֹל בֹּכִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: My harp also is turned to mourning, and my organ into the voice of them that weep.
verses.rendering:
My harp is tuned to mourning,
and my flute to the sound of weeping.
verses.expanded_rendering: The chapter closes with two instruments — kinnor (harp, lyre) and ugav (pipe, flute) — that represent the full range of musical expression. Both are now set to a single key: grief. The kinnor is the instrument David played before Saul, the instrument of psalms and celebration. The ugav appears in Genesis 4:21 as one of the first inventions of civilization. Job's statement that both are turned to mourning means that every form of self-expression, every channel of beauty and art in his life, now produces only the sound of pain. This is the final image before Job's oath of innocence in chapter 31 — the man who once made widows' hearts sing (29:13) can now produce only weeping.
translator_notes: The kinnor (lyre, harp) is Israel's quintessential instrument of joy and worship. The ugav (pipe, flute) is associated with pastoral and festive settings. Le-evel ('to mourning') and le-qol bokhim ('to the voice of weeping') transform instruments of celebration into instruments of lament. The verse deliberately echoes Psalm 137:2 in reverse: there the exiles hang up their harps because they cannot sing; here Job's harp still plays, but it can only mourn.
